questions.prompt: Let's clear this one up: how do AWS Regions and Availability Zones relate?

answers.text: Short version: A Region contains multiple isolated Availability Zones.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. A Region contains multiple isolated Availability Zones.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. A Region contains multiple isolated Availability Zones.

answers.text: I'd go with this: Regions are the larger geographic units.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Regions are the larger geographic units.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Regions are the larger geographic units.

answers.text: I think the important part is this: deploying across zones can reduce some single-location failure risks.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Deploying across zones can reduce some single-location failure risks.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Deploying across zones can reduce some single-location failure risks.

questions.prompt: Curious where people land on this: what does least privilege mean in AWS IAM?

answers.text: My answer: Give every application administrator access and rely on logs.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Broad permissions increase the impact of mistakes and compromise.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Broad permissions increase the impact of mistakes and compromise.

answers.text: I'd phrase it like this: Permissions should be reviewed as requirements change.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Permissions should be reviewed as requirements change.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Permissions should be reviewed as requirements change.

answers.text: The way I understand it: grant only the permissions needed for the intended task.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Grant only the permissions needed for the intended task.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Grant only the permissions needed for the intended task.

questions.prompt: This keeps starting arguments: what kind of service is Amazon S3?

answers.text: The practical answer is: S3 folders are real filesystem directories that contain objects.

feedback.legit: Not this one. S3 is a flat object store; folder-like views are derived from key prefixes.
feedback.sus: Good catch. S3 is a flat object store; folder-like views are derived from key prefixes.

answers.text: My answer: An object storage service.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. An object storage service.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. An object storage service.

answers.text: I'd phrase it like this: A block device mounted directly by every EC2 instance.

feedback.legit: Not this one. S3 exposes object APIs rather than acting as a normal block device.
feedback.sus: Good catch. S3 exposes object APIs rather than acting as a normal block device.

questions.prompt: I've seen mixed takes, so: how do EC2 security groups treat response traffic?

answers.text: I think the important part is this: they are stateful, so allowed inbound traffic permits its response.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. They are stateful, so allowed inbound traffic permits its response.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. They are stateful, so allowed inbound traffic permits its response.

answers.text: Unless I'm mixing things up, stateful connection tracking permits return traffic.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Stateful connection tracking permits return traffic.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Stateful connection tracking permits return traffic.

answers.text: This is what trips people up: Rules allow traffic; security groups do not contain explicit deny rules.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Rules allow traffic; security groups do not contain explicit deny rules.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Rules allow traffic; security groups do not contain explicit deny rules.

questions.prompt: No searching: how do network ACLs differ from security groups?

answers.text: This is what trips people up: Network ACLs are attached only to individual IAM users.

feedback.legit: Not this one. They filter traffic at subnet boundaries.
feedback.sus: Good catch. They filter traffic at subnet boundaries.

answers.text: This was the explanation that clicked for me: They can contain both allow and deny rules.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. They can contain both allow and deny rules.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. They can contain both allow and deny rules.

answers.text: Pretty sure this is the key: Network ACLs are stateless subnet-level controls.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Network ACLs are stateless subnet-level controls.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Network ACLs are stateless subnet-level controls.

questions.prompt: Someone dropped this in the group chat: does AWS Lambda remove every operational concern?

answers.text: The way I understand it: the platform manages servers while the customer manages function code and configuration.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. The platform manages servers while the customer manages function code and configuration.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. The platform manages servers while the customer manages function code and configuration.

answers.text: Short version: Yes, serverless means there are no limits or failures to design for.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Functions still have quotas, timeouts, dependencies, and failure modes.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Functions still have quotas, timeouts, dependencies, and failure modes.

answers.text: I'd go with this: Cold starts can affect latency for some workloads.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Cold starts can affect latency for some workloads.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Cold starts can affect latency for some workloads.

questions.prompt: Curious where people land on this: what is the main purpose of a traditional RDS Multi-AZ DB instance deployment?

answers.text: My answer: Improve availability through a standby and managed failover.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Improve availability through a standby and managed failover.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Improve availability through a standby and managed failover.

answers.text: I'd phrase it like this: Automatically scale read traffic across the standby.

feedback.legit: Not this one. A Multi-AZ DB instance standby is for availability and does not serve read traffic.
feedback.sus: Good catch. A Multi-AZ DB instance standby is for availability and does not serve read traffic.

answers.text: The way I understand it: a Multi-AZ failover is guaranteed to cause zero application interruption.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Failover can interrupt connections, so applications need retry and recovery behavior.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Failover can interrupt connections, so applications need retry and recovery behavior.

questions.prompt: Let's clear this one up: why does DynamoDB partition-key design matter?

answers.text: Short version: A constant partition key maximizes horizontal scaling.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Concentrating traffic on one key can create a hot partition.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Concentrating traffic on one key can create a hot partition.

answers.text: I'd go with this: Access patterns should guide primary-key and index design.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Access patterns should guide primary-key and index design.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Access patterns should guide primary-key and index design.

answers.text: I think the important part is this: it influences how data and traffic are distributed across partitions.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. It influences how data and traffic are distributed across partitions.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. It influences how data and traffic are distributed across partitions.

questions.prompt: Before I confidently say the wrong thing: what does Amazon CloudFront provide?

answers.text: I'd phrase it like this: CloudFront can deliver only static files and cannot proxy dynamic requests.

feedback.legit: Not this one. CloudFront can forward dynamic requests to origins as well as cache eligible content.
feedback.sus: Good catch. CloudFront can forward dynamic requests to origins as well as cache eligible content.

answers.text: The way I understand it: a content delivery network with edge caching.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. A content delivery network with edge caching.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. A content delivery network with edge caching.

answers.text: Short version: A replacement for IAM authorization in every application.

feedback.legit: Not this one. CloudFront distributes content; application authorization remains a separate concern.
feedback.sus: Good catch. CloudFront distributes content; application authorization remains a separate concern.

questions.prompt: Before I confidently say the wrong thing: should an Amazon SQS Standard queue consumer handle duplicate messages?

answers.text: I'd phrase it like this: Yes, consumers should be idempotent because messages can be delivered more than once.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Yes, consumers should be idempotent because messages can be delivered more than once.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Yes, consumers should be idempotent because messages can be delivered more than once.

answers.text: The way I understand it: no, Standard queues guarantee exactly-once processing.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Standard queues use at-least-once delivery, so duplicate delivery is possible.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Standard queues use at-least-once delivery, so duplicate delivery is possible.

answers.text: Short version: A visibility timeout is not the same as deleting a successfully processed message.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. A visibility timeout is not the same as deleting a successfully processed message.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. A visibility timeout is not the same as deleting a successfully processed message.

questions.prompt: Before I confidently say the wrong thing: what does the AWS shared responsibility model divide?

answers.text: I'd phrase it like this: AWS is responsible for all customer data classification and IAM policy choices.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Customers retain responsibility for their data and access configuration.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Customers retain responsibility for their data and access configuration.

answers.text: The way I understand it: the exact division changes with the type of managed service.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. The exact division changes with the type of managed service.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. The exact division changes with the type of managed service.

answers.text: Short version: AWS secures the cloud infrastructure while customers secure their workloads and configurations.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. AWS secures the cloud infrastructure while customers secure their workloads and configurations.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. AWS secures the cloud infrastructure while customers secure their workloads and configurations.

questions.prompt: Someone dropped this in the group chat: can automatic scaling alone guarantee application availability?

answers.text: The way I understand it: automatic scaling creates new healthy capacity instantly when traffic rises.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Detection, instance launch, and health checks take time, so scaling is not instantaneous.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Detection, instance launch, and health checks take time, so scaling is not instantaneous.

answers.text: Short version: Yes, adding instances fixes every failure mode.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Scaling does not repair bad dependencies, unhealthy code, or flawed routing.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Scaling does not repair bad dependencies, unhealthy code, or flawed routing.

answers.text: I'd go with this: Scaling an EC2 group automatically scales every database dependency as well.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Each dependency needs its own capacity and scaling design; scaling one tier does not scale the others.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Each dependency needs its own capacity and scaling design; scaling one tier does not scale the others.
